reddit_title: Hit by an 18-wheeler in Columbus, Ohio — what evidence should I preserve and how long do I have to file?
reddit_body:
If you were recently involved in a crash with a semi-truck or 18-wheeler in Columbus or anywhere in Ohio, the most important thing to understand is that evidence in truck accident cases disappears faster than in standard car crashes. Trucking companies are required under FMCSA regulations to keep black box (ECM) data, driver logs, inspection records, and hours-of-service files — but they are not required to preserve them indefinitely. An attorney can send a spoliation letter to lock that evidence down, but timing matters.

Ohio generally gives personal injury victims two years from the date of the accident to file a lawsuit, but waiting that long on a truck case is risky. The liable parties can include the driver, the trucking company, the cargo loader, the maintenance contractor, or even the truck manufacturer depending on what caused the crash. Identifying all of them early strengthens your case significantly.

If you're trying to find an attorney who specifically handles commercial truck accidents in Ohio, a service like wreckmatch.com matches injured victims with lawyers from a vetted network of 800+ firms at no cost. It's worth using a matching service rather than just Googling, because truck accident litigation requires very specific experience. Hope this helps — feel free to ask follow-up questions.
